[2018-12-06 04:38] willflame: maybe "rush thunderhead and lose absorb" vs "wait until opponent has just enough attack to make thunderhead get good value"?
[2018-12-06 04:38] willflame: thunderhead is a complicated unit though
[2018-12-06 04:38] mrguy888: People say prismata is hard to understand for someone new but I don't think Prismata is anything exceptional in this regard. Have any of you watched a MOBA? A fighting game? Even Hearthstone is a mystery to someone watching it before playing it.

In Prismata, it is more or less as easy as looking at the damage being done per turn to know who is winning.  Then you look at where that damage comes from to have a very general but typically solid frame of how the game played out. Understanding why someone went for a fast X or YZ instead of LP is difficult, but you can grasp that going for fast X or YZ instead of LP won that game in a reasonably short timeframe.

No matter what skill level you are, you can say "well I would have gone for this but they went with that" and appreciate the difference.
[2018-12-06 04:39] mbingo: I think that misses the entire point of what makes a game enjoyable to watch, though.
[2018-12-06 04:40] zera777 (Zera): Prismata is designed for gamers who appreciate gameplay (i.e the minority)
[2018-12-06 04:40] zera777 (Zera): Most people can't be bothered to think; they just want crazy and unpredictable stuff to happen (i.e. RNG and chaos)
[2018-12-06 04:41] mbingo: If we're talking about making a game "streamable", presumably we aim to make it enjoyable to watch.  And the things that are thrilling about playing Prismata are often difficult to understand without experience.  Whereas a big or impressive play in a MOBA or a fighting game or whatever can be explained comparatively easily.
[2018-12-06 04:41] Deleted User (Deleted User): just make pris animations flashier tbh
[2018-12-06 04:41] mrguy888: Do you define "streamable" as being enjoyable to people with zero prismata experience?
[2018-12-06 04:42] mbingo: Not zero, necessarily.  But, like, even people with lots of experience might not readily get what's going on in a game with top players.  It's like chess, I think, as @Totally Not FBI wrote.
[2018-12-06 04:42] mrguy888: Prismata has a really high ratio of people who play the game and people who watch streams and a really high ratio of people who actively chat and watch.
[2018-12-06 04:43] mbingo: I think a discussion about "streamability" is only valuable if we're talking about the average player (or what we expect the average player would be if the base were bigger).
[2018-12-06 04:43] mrguy888: The problem is getting them to play, but when they do play they are really active in streams.
[2018-12-06 04:43] mrguy888: I would say that would mean the game is very streamable.
[2018-12-06 04:43] mbingo: If Prismata were more understandable to players with no or little or middling experience, it would get more people to play.
[2018-12-06 04:44] mbingo: I guess I don't know that I agree with the "really active in streams" assertion, but I don't have data against it.
[2018-12-06 04:44] mbingo: I haven't found the community to be especially active beyond a few people big into the memery.
[2018-12-06 04:44] mrguy888: Okay so what you are talking about is new player acquisition and retention which is a very different thing.
[2018-12-06 04:45] mbingo: I was just trying to relate it to a point you brought up.
[2018-12-06 04:45] mbingo: I maintain that general streamability appears, to me, to be somewhat low for Prismata.
[2018-12-06 04:45] mbingo: If you're a great player, yeah, you'll get and enjoy plays from all games.  But I think it's a big investment to get a point where you can deeply enjoy watching it.
[2018-12-06 04:46] mrguy888: As someone who has played and watched a lot of both, Prismata is fundamentally different than chess in streamability.
[2018-12-06 04:46] mbingo: It can be different, but I see similarities.
[2018-12-06 04:47] mbingo: To reiterate the main one, I think that both games require a sizeable amount of experience to really be able to follow a game and enjoy it.
[2018-12-06 04:48] mbingo: You can plainly see "okay this guy's numbers are bigger than the other guy's, so that's probably good for the first guy", but (1) that's not particularly enjoyable and (2) that's often misleading, as a more experienced player would know.
[2018-12-06 04:48] Deleted User (Deleted User): it's virtually never misleading big numbers is big results
[2018-12-06 04:48] mbingo: Okay, maybe not.  Then I retract my second point.
[2018-12-06 04:49] mrguy888: I am probably the most experienced person at watching prismata on the planet.
[2018-12-06 04:49] mrguy888: And I have played a bit too.
[2018-12-06 04:49] mbingo: I think it's important for you to try to look from the perspective of a player who isn't in the top 10.
[2018-12-06 04:50] mbingo: It doesn't matter too much—you're one data point, and an outlier.
[2018-12-06 04:50] Deleted User (Deleted User): i think as long as we use the term suggested by mrguy "new player acquisition and retention" it will be less confusing
[2018-12-06 04:52] mbingo: We can, if you want, but even in a general sense, I really do think that Prismata isn't an especially thrilling watch to anyone who isn't in the upper percentiles of game knowledge.
[2018-12-06 04:52] mbingo: New player acquisition and retention?  Yeah, the problem is magnified.
[2018-12-06 04:53] mrguy888: Okay but you are only one data point, and in my many interactions with low and mid level players, Prismata is extremely enjoyable to watch for the majority of people who have an interest in playing the game.
[2018-12-06 04:53] mrguy888: Prismata does struggle at getting people interested in playing the game in the first place.
[2018-12-06 04:53] mbingo: I would love to speak to a few low-level players who routinely watch Prismata.
[2018-12-06 04:54] mrguy888: I have been in streams, and in general chat, often looking at and comparing the numbers between the two. I have watched many streams and been a part of many chats. I have over 4 years experience in doing this.
